content:
Hydrothermally robust carbon-silica-zirconia ceramic membranes for efficient pervaporation dehydration Xinpu Niu , Norihiro Moriyama , Hiroki Nagasawa , Toshinori Tsuru , Masakoto Kanezashi * Chemical Engineering Program, Graduate School of Advanced Science and Engineering, Hiroshima University, Higashi-Hiroshima, 739-8527, Japan

A R T I C L E I N F O Keywords:Membrane separationCarbon-silica-zirconia ceramic membranesPervaporation dehydrationHydrothermal stability

citation.title: Hydrothermally robust carbon-silica-zirconia ceramic membranes for efficient pervaporation dehydration

content: A B S T R A C T Silica-derived membranes offer significant potential for pervaporation (PV) dehydration applications due to their distinct sieving properties and tunable chemical structures. However, simultaneously enhancing hydrothermal stability, hydrophilicity, and porosity remains a formidable challenge. In this study, carbon-SiO2-ZrO2 ceramic composite membranes were fabricated by calcining organic chelating ligand-modified SiO2–ZrO2 (APTES-ZrTBGA, AZG) precursors under an inert atmosphere. The AZG-550 membrane, carbonized at 550 ◦C, exhibits an optimal balance of high microporosity and moderate hydrophilicity, resulting in outstanding PV dehydration performance governed by both adsorption-diffusion and molecular sieving mechanisms. During the PV dehy­dration of H2O/MeOH (10/90 wt %) and H2O/EtOH (10/90 wt %), the membrane achieved H2O permeances exceeding 10-6 mol m-2 s-1 Pa-1, with H2O/MeOH and H2O/EtOH selectivities of 140 and 2000, respectively. Moreover, the free carbon derived from the calcination process played a crucial role in preserving the integrity of the SiO2–ZrO2 network, thereby significantly enhancing the hydrothermal stability of the membrane. This study provides important insights into the design of high-performance silica-derived membranes for PV dehydration, emphasizing the potential of carbon incorporation to improve both stability and membrane performance.

citation.title: Hydrothermally robust carbon-silica-zirconia ceramic membranes for efficient pervaporation dehydration

content: 1. Introduction The global pursuit of a green and sustainable future has intensified efforts to advance separation technologies, aiming to enhance energy efficiency, minimize waste, and achieve zero discharge. The integration of energy and process optimization, coupled with the recovery and recycling of valuable products from waste streams, is pivotal in realizing this objective. Among various approaches, membrane-based separation technologies have garnered significant attention due to their energy efficiency, particularly in the context of the growing imperative to reduce carbon emissions and mitigate environmental pollution [1,2]. Pervaporation (PV), a membrane-based separation method, has garnered significant attention for its effectiveness in separating azeo­tropic mixtures, thermally unstable or thermally sensitive compounds, and mixtures containing components with close boiling points [3–5]. Compared to conventional separation processes such as distillation and absorption, PV offers superior energy efficiency, as it requires latent heat of evaporation only for the permeate components [6]. Furthermore, mild operating conditions, minimal environmental impact, and the absence of additional chemical agents in the feed reinforce its potential as a sustainable separation technology. Among its diverse applications, the PV dehydration of organic solvents is projected to be increasingly sought after, particularly for alcohol purification [7,8].

citation.title: Hydrothermally robust carbon-silica-zirconia ceramic membranes for efficient pervaporation dehydration

content: iverse applications, the PV dehydration of organic solvents is projected to be increasingly sought after, particularly for alcohol purification [7,8]. The rational design of membrane materials and structures to achieve optimal separation performance is recognized as a crucial strategy for advancing the industrial implementation of PV processes [9]. In this context, recent advancements have emphasized a shift in the approach towards developing novel membrane materials with enhanced separa­tion efficiency, as well as superior chemical and thermal stability, to enable effective solvent dehydration under elevated operating temper­atures. Various types of polymeric membranes including poly (vinyl alcohol) (PVA), chitosan, alginate-based, and polyimide (PI) mem­branes, have been developed for PV dehydration, with some achieving commercial viability [10–13]. Furthermore, to overcome the challenges associated with swelling and stability in conventional polymeric mem­branes, significant advances have been made in recent decades with the development of inorganic sub-nanoporous membranes, such as carbon molecular sieves (CMS), zeolites, and amorphous silica, which exhibit enhanced durability and performance [14]. CMS membranes have attracted significant interest in PV dehydration applications due to their

citation.title: Hydrothermally robust carbon-silica-zirconia ceramic membranes for efficient pervaporation dehydration

content: Available online 7 May 2025 0376-7388/ 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC license ( http://creativecommons.org/licenses/bync/4.0/ ). simple fabrication processes and robust stability under harsh operating conditions. However, the development of ultrathin, defect-free CMS membranes with high PV efficiency presents a considerable challenge [15,16]. Zeolite membranes have demonstrated exceptional selectivity and permeability for the dehydration of alcohol/water mixtures. Notably, A-type and T-type zeolites have been successfully commer­cialized for PV dehydration applications, highlighting their practical viability in industrial settings [17]. Nevertheless, the development of advanced membranes that simultaneously achieve high separation ef­ficiency and superior stability, particularly those capable of operating effectively with high H2O content in PV dehydration, remains a critical focus for focus for advancement in this field. Amorphous silica has emerged as a promising material for PV dehydration due to its exceptional chemical resistance and favorable high performance in both gas and liquid separation processes [18,19]. The micropores structure (0.3–0.4 nm) of siloxane-based amorphous silica membranes exhibits pronounced molecular-sieving ability, enabling selective permeation of water (H2O) molecules (0.2955 nm) while effectively rejecting larger species [18].

citation.title: Hydrothermally robust carbon-silica-zirconia ceramic membranes for efficient pervaporation dehydration

content: nounced molecular-sieving ability, enabling selective permeation of water (H2O) molecules (0.2955 nm) while effectively rejecting larger species [18]. However, the inherent structural disorder and relative flexibility of conventional amorphous silica, along with the presence of (Si–O)xSi-OH defects, compromise its hydrothermal stability [20]. Under elevated temperatures and humidity conditions, the network structure is prone to hydrolysis and densifica­tion, which significantly restricts its long-term practical applicability. Various strategies have been proposed to address this challenge, including the development of organic-inorganic hybrid silica mem­branes (organosilica membranes) and composition with metal oxides (composite ceramic membranes), etc. [19,21,22] Organosilica mem­branes have gained significant attention due to their exceptional sta­bility in pervaporation (PV) dehydration applications [23]. This enhanced stability is primarily attributed to the incorporation of hy­drolytically stable hydrophobic groups into the network structure, which effectively shield siloxane bonds from water molecules, thereby protecting them from hydrolysis. However, the incorporation of hy­drophobic groups is not preferable to the H2O permeance enhancement referring to the adsorption-diffusion mechanism, since the hydrophi­licity of membrane plays a crucial role in determining separation per­formance [23,24].

citation.title: Hydrothermally robust carbon-silica-zirconia ceramic membranes for efficient pervaporation dehydration

content: ring to the adsorption-diffusion mechanism, since the hydrophi­licity of membrane plays a crucial role in determining separation per­formance [23,24]. Consequently, the development of highly stable hydrophilic silica membranes with high H2O permeation for PV dehy­dration remains an area of ongoing research. The fabrication of composite ceramic membranes by integration silica with stable metal oxides, particularly zirconia (ZrO2), via the solgel method presents a promising strategy for improving hydrothermal stability [25,26]. However, the ZrO2 phase is prone to agglomeration due to the rapid hydrolysis of the ZrO2 precursor during the sol-gel process, which reduces the porosity of the obtained membrane, adversely affects the permeance, and compromises hydrothermal sta­bility under water-rich conditions [25]. The modification of ZrO2 pre­cursors with organic chelating ligands (OCLs) has been demonstrated to effectively reduce the hydrolysis rate by consuming terminal alkoxy groups, thereby facilitating the formation of a more uniform composite ceramic structure [27]. Moreover, the carbon-contained ceramic mem­branes (carbon-SiO2-ZrO2), derived from the carbonization of OCLs within the SiO2–ZrO2 composite, provide an opportunity to combine CMS with ceramic membranes. These membranes have demonstrated enhanced porosity and a uniform pore size distribution, leading to improved membrane permeance and molecular sieving properties [28].

citation.title: Hydrothermally robust carbon-silica-zirconia ceramic membranes for efficient pervaporation dehydration

content: ave demonstrated enhanced porosity and a uniform pore size distribution, leading to improved membrane permeance and molecular sieving properties [28]. Additionally, the carbonization of organic components is expected to increase the membrane’s hydrophilicity, further enhancing its perfor­mance in PV dehydration applications. These results suggest that car­bon-SiO2-ZrO2 membranes hold significant potential as promising candidates for highly efficient PV dehydration. However, to date, no relevant studies have been conducted to investigate their full potential and applicability. In this study, we explored the application of carbon-based ceramic composite membranes for PV dehydration while systematically investigating the impact of membrane surface chemistry and micro­structure on PV dehydration performance. The carbon-SiO2-ZrO2 membranes were fabricated using the APTES-ZrTB-GA (AZG) sol, derived from 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), zirconium (IV) tert-butoxide (ZrTB), and glyoxylic acid (GA). The rigid cross-linked organic structure, formed through aldimine condensation between APTES and GA, enhanced the dispersion uniformity of the free carbon within the ceramic network, effectively mitigating defect formation and improving membrane integrity [29]. The PV dehydration performance of organic solvents (methanol, ethanol, acetone, and isopropanol) for the obtained carbonized AZG membranes was systematically evaluated.

citation.title: Hydrothermally robust carbon-silica-zirconia ceramic membranes for efficient pervaporation dehydration

content: performance of organic solvents (methanol, ethanol, acetone, and isopropanol) for the obtained carbonized AZG membranes was systematically evaluated. Notably, the AZG membrane carbonized at 550 ◦C (AZG-550) exhibited exceptional PV dehydration performance, which was attributed to the synergistic effects of its hydrophilicity and well-defined microporous structure. Furthermore, we demonstrated the remarkable stability of carbon-SiO2-ZrO2 membranes under high H2O content and hydrother­mal conditions. Additionally, we elucidated the role of carbon within the ceramic network in enhancing the hydrothermal stability of the fabri­cated membranes. 2. Experimental 2.1. Materials Porous α-alumina (Al2O3) tubular supports (60 % porosity, 10 cm in length, 1 cm outer diameter, and 1.2 μm pore size) were obtained from Nikkato Co., Japan. Methanol (MeOH, 99.5 %), ethanol (EtOH, 99.5 %), acetone (99.5 %), isopropanol (IPA, 99.5 %), and glyoxylic acid mon­ohydrate (GA, 95 %) were sourced from FUJIFILM Pure Chemical Cor­poration (Japan). Acetic acid (99.0 %) and nitric acid (HNO3, 60 %) were purchased from Nacalai Tesque, Inc. (Japan). The Si precursor, 3aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES, >98.0 %), and Zr precursor, zir­conium (IV) tert-butoxide (ZrTB, 80 wt% in 1-butanol), were acquired from TCI and Sigma-Aldrich, respectively. Tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) and yttrium nitrate hexahydrate (Y(NO3)3.6H2O) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich for the intermediate layer sol preparation. 2.2.

citation.title: Hydrothermally robust carbon-silica-zirconia ceramic membranes for efficient pervaporation dehydration

content: aethoxysilane (TEOS) and yttrium nitrate hexahydrate (Y(NO3)3.6H2O) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich for the intermediate layer sol preparation. 2.2. Sol and membrane preparation In this study, a 5.0 wt % APTES-ZrTB-GA sol with crosslinked organic moieties (denoted as AZG) was prepared following the previously re­ported procedure [29]. Initially, ZrTB was modified with GA in EtOH at a GA/ZrTB molar ratio of 4:1. Subsequently, an APTES solution in EtOH was gradually added to the GA-modified ZrTB solution, while main­taining an APTES/ZrTB molar ratio of 4:1. The mixture was thoroughly stirred, followed by the addition of H2O and acetic acid. To facilitate hydrolysis and polycondensation, the resulting solution was stirred at room temperature at 550 rpm for 12 h. The alkoxide/H2O/acetic acid ratio was controlled at 1:4:0.3. The xerogel powders derived from AZG sols were prepared through slow solvent evaporation at 50–80 ◦C in air, followed by carbonization under an N2 atmosphere at the temperature of the membrane fabrica­tion, denoting as AZG-x, where x represents the value of calcination temperature. The membrane fabrication process has been previously described [29]. In detail, the obtained membrane comprises the following layers: the porous α-Al2O3 substrate, particle layer, intermediate layer, and separation layer. Porous tubular α-Al2O3 was selected as the membrane substrate.

citation.title: Hydrothermally robust carbon-silica-zirconia ceramic membranes for efficient pervaporation dehydration

content: the porous α-Al2O3 substrate, particle layer, intermediate layer, and separation layer. Porous tubular α-Al2O3 was selected as the membrane substrate. For particle layer fabrication, a nonwoven fabric was first wetted with a 10 wt % dispersion of 1–2 μm α-Al2O3 particles in Y–SiO2–ZrO2 sol, which was prepared following previously reported procedures [30], and then lightly coated onto the substrate. After drying at room temperature, the coated substrate was calcined at 700 ◦C in an air atmosphere for 20 min. The α-Al2O3 particles were bonded to the substrate through the colloidal Y–SiO2–ZrO2 binder. This process was repeated 3 times to ensure uniform particle coverage. Subsequently, a

citation.title: Hydrothermally robust carbon-silica-zirconia ceramic membranes for efficient pervaporation dehydration

content: 10 wt % dispersion of 0.2 μm α-Al2O3 particles in Y–SiO2–ZrO2 sol was coated onto the substrate surface via the same method, and this process was also repeated 3 times. The particle layer effectively covered the pinholes and defects on the substrate, resulting in a smooth surface. The intermediate layer was fabricated using pure Y–SiO2–ZrO2 sol, which was diluted to 0.5 wt % with deionized H2O. The substrate with the particle layer was first heated to 200 ◦C. A nonwoven fabric wetted with diluted Y–SiO2–ZrO2 sol was lightly coated onto the substrate. After coating, the membranes were calcined at 700 ◦C in air for 20 min. This step was repeated 5–6 times to obtain a smooth intermediate layer with a pore size of approximately 1 nm to prevent the penetration of the AZG sol. This multiple coating process promoted the formation of a smooth, defect-free separation layer. Finally, the selective layer was obtained by coating a 0.2 wt % EtOH-diluted AZG sol. The substrate was first heated to 200 ◦C and then lightly coated with AZG sol-wetted nonwoven fabric, followed by carbonization at 550 or 700 ◦C in a N2 atmosphere for 30 min. This process was repeated 5 times to obtain carbon-SiO2-ZrO2 membranes. The characterization methods are detailed in Text S1 of the Supporting Information. 2.3. Gas and PV performance measurement The single-gas permeation performance of AZG membranes was evaluated using He, H2, CO2, N2, CH4, and SF6 over a temperature range of 50–200 ◦C.

citation.title: Hydrothermally robust carbon-silica-zirconia ceramic membranes for efficient pervaporation dehydration

content: rement The single-gas permeation performance of AZG membranes was evaluated using He, H2, CO2, N2, CH4, and SF6 over a temperature range of 50–200 ◦C. The experimental setup is schematically depicted in Fig. S1a. The pressure difference across the membrane was maintained at approximately 200 kPa using a backpressure valve to ensure stable measurement conditions. The gas flow of the permeate side was measured using a soap film meter. Before measurement, the membrane was placed in the membrane module and heated to 200 ◦C for over 12 h under a He flow of 20 mL/min to remove any adsorbed H2O. All tests were conducted at least 5 times to ensure system stability, with de­viations maintained within 5 %. The schematic of the PV setup is presented in Fig. S1b. The feeding temperature was controlled using a thermocouple and a heater coil, ranging from 30 to 60 ◦C for H2O/MeOH mixtures and 40–70 ◦C for H2O/EtOH mixtures. Aqueous solutions of the organic solvents (the physical properties of the organic solvents used in this study, including MeOH, EtOH, acetone, and IPA, are listed in Table S1) were continu­ously stirred outside the membrane at an absolute pressure of 101.3 kPa, while the interior absolute pressure of the membrane was maintained close to 0 kPa. The permeate components were collected in a cold trap, and both the feed and permeate compositions were analyzed gas chro­matography (GC-14B, Shimadzu Corp., Japan).

citation.title: Hydrothermally robust carbon-silica-zirconia ceramic membranes for efficient pervaporation dehydration

citation.title: Hydrothermally robust carbon-silica-zirconia ceramic membranes for efficient pervaporation dehydration

content: (mol m-2 s-1), Pp,i (Pa), P0,i (Pa), γi ( -), and xi ( -) represent the permeance, selectivity, flux, partial pressure in the permeate side, saturated vapor pressure in the feed, activity coefficient, and mole fraction of component i, respectively. 3. Results and discussion 3.1. Effect of carbonization on AZG hybrid material chemical structure and surface properties The thermal decomposition behavior and stability of the AZG sample was evaluated using TGA/DTG. Prior to testing, the AZG xerogel powder sample was pretreated at 100 ◦C under an N2 flow for 2 h to remove any adsorbed moisture. As illustrated in Fig. 1a, the onset of weight loss commenced at approximately 200 ◦C, corresponding to the degradation of unhydrolyzed butoxy groups and the partial decomposition of organic moieties within the ceramic structure [31]. The maximum weight loss rate was observed at 435 ◦C, followed by a gradual decrease, stabilizing at approximately 670 ◦C. This trend indicates the significant degrada­tion of the organic group and largely developed carbonized structure between 435 and 670 ◦C. After calcination at 670 ◦C, all organic moi­eties were transformed into well-developed carbon structures. Based on these observations, carbonization temperatures of 550 ◦C and 700 ◦C were selected as representative conditions for fabricating carbon-­SiO2-ZrO2 composite materials and membranes. Fig. 1b presents the FTIR spectra of AZG films carbonized at 550 ◦C and 700 ◦C.

citation.title: Hydrothermally robust carbon-silica-zirconia ceramic membranes for efficient pervaporation dehydration

content: s for fabricating carbon-­SiO2-ZrO2 composite materials and membranes. Fig. 1b presents the FTIR spectra of AZG films carbonized at 550 ◦C and 700 ◦C. The intense characteristic peak centered at approximately 1020 cm-1, corresponding to Si–O–Si asymmetric stretching, indicates that the Si–O–Si bond angle is less than 144◦[32]. Additionally, the characteristic peak at 1550 cm-1, attributed to the C––C bond, was observed in both samples and is associated with the skeletal vibrations of sp2 carbon domains within the graphitic structure [33]. This suggests that calcination at 550 ◦C is sufficient to convert the organic precursors into graphitic carbon. Notably, the intensity of this peak slightly increased in the AZG-700 sample, with the C––C/Si–O–Si peak area ratio increasing from 0.02 for AZG-550 to 0.05 for AZG-700. This suggests that the higher calcination temperature promotes the formation of a more highly ordered graphitic carbonized structure. As reported in our previous studies, the amine groups on APTES and the aldehyde groups on GA undergo condensation to form imine bonds, characterized by an absorption band corresponding to the C––N bond at approximately 1650 cm-1 [29,34]. Notably, even after carbonization at 550 ◦C and 700 ◦C, the C––N peak remains detectable, accompanied by the emer­gence of a distinct C–N peak at 1460 cm-1 [35]. These results indicate that the N element within the imine bond is preserved even after carbonization at 700 ◦C.

citation.title: Hydrothermally robust carbon-silica-zirconia ceramic membranes for efficient pervaporation dehydration

content: distinct C–N peak at 1460 cm-1 [35]. These results indicate that the N element within the imine bond is preserved even after carbonization at 700 ◦C. To further visualize the chemical structure of the free carbon generated during calcination, Raman spectroscopy was conducted on the AZG films. As shown in Fig. 1c, the Raman spectra of both AZG-550 and AZG-700 exhibited the D peak at 1340 cm-1 and the G peak at 1580 cm-1, corresponding to disordered carbon and highly oriented graphitic carbon, respectively [36]. Additionally, the intensity ratio of the D band to the G band (ID/IG) serves as a key parameter for evaluating the evo­lution of graphite-like carbon structures. The ID/IG ratios for AZG-550 and AZG-700 were determined to be 1.09 and 0.96, respectively, exhibiting a slight reduction with increasing carbonization temperature. This trend suggests an enhanced degree of graphitization at elevated calcination temperatures, consistent with the findings from the FTIR results. Upon confirming the chemical structure of carbon at varying calci­nation temperatures, the N chemical states in the hybrid materials were investigated through high-resolution N 1s XPS. As illustrated in Fig. 1d, the N 1s spectrum for AZG-550 can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 399.1 eV, 400.5 eV, and 402.5 eV, corresponding to the C––N, C–N, and NSiO2, respectively [37].

citation.title: Hydrothermally robust carbon-silica-zirconia ceramic membranes for efficient pervaporation dehydration

content: um for AZG-550 can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 399.1 eV, 400.5 eV, and 402.5 eV, corresponding to the C––N, C–N, and NSiO2, respectively [37]. In contrast, the N 1s spectrum of AZG-700 exhibited a shift toward lower binding energies, with deconvoluted peaks at 398.8 eV, 399.8 eV, and 402.5 eV, which are attributed to

citation.title: Hydrothermally robust carbon-silica-zirconia ceramic membranes for efficient pervaporation dehydration

content:
X. Niu et al. Journal of Membrane Science 730 (2025) 124197 pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and NSiO2, respectively [38]. These findings suggest that N-doped graphitic carbon structures are likely formed within the AZG network following high-temperature calcination. The structural transformations of the hybrid ceramic at different calcination temperatures are illustrated in Fig. 2. Typically, as the calcination temperature increases, the terminal hydroxyl groups of SiO2–ZrO2 ceramics undergo hydrolysis and condensation, promoting a denser network structure [24]. Simultaneously, the organic components in AZG undergo pyrolysis, facilitating the formation of partially evolved N-doped carbon structures at 550 ◦C. At 700 ◦C, the carbon structure further graphitized, with N in the graphitic carbon transitioning into pyridinic and pyrrolic N species. Notably, compared to the moderate hydrophilicity of graphene, N-doped graphene exhibits significantly enhanced hydrophilic properties, which is expected to improve the PV performance of the obtained membrane [39]. Following the elucidation of chemical structural changes at different

Fig. 1. (a) TGA and DTG curves of AZG xerogel powder. (b) FT-IR spectra of AZG-550 and AZG-700 films. (c) Raman spectra of AZG-550 and AZG-700 films. (d) N 1s XPS spectra of AZG-550 and AZG-700 xerogel powders.

citation.title: Hydrothermally robust carbon-silica-zirconia ceramic membranes for efficient pervaporation dehydration

content: Fig. 2. Schematic representation of the structural evolution during the carbonization of AZG composite ceramic material. X. Niu et al. Journal of Membrane Science 730 (2025) 124197 calcination temperatures, the microstructure and surface properties were further examined. Fig. 3a presents the CO2 adsorption/desorption isotherms, which are highly sensitive for detecting ultramicropores (<0.6 nm) and pyridinic/pyrrolic groups, with which they specific in­teractions [40]. Apparently, AZG-550 exhibited a significantly higher CO2 adsorption capacity than AZG-700 across the entire pressure range, indicating the formation of a more microporous structure at 550 ◦C carbonization. In contrast, carbonization at 700 ◦C resulted in a denser microstructure. Although 700 ◦C carbonization is expected to enhance the pyrolysis of organic components and release network space, the lower porosity of AZG-700 indicates that the densification of the ceramic network structure plays a more significant role in pore structure alter­ation of the AZG-700 sample. Notably, AZG-550 exhibited an almost reversible CO2 adsorption/desorption isotherm, while AZG-700 displays an irreversible isotherm with a hysteresis loop. This distinction can be attributed to the presence of pyridinic and pyrrolic N species in the AZG-700 structure, as evidenced by XPS analysis, which revealed a strong affinity for CO2 [41].

citation.title: Hydrothermally robust carbon-silica-zirconia ceramic membranes for efficient pervaporation dehydration

content: he presence of pyridinic and pyrrolic N species in the AZG-700 structure, as evidenced by XPS analysis, which revealed a strong affinity for CO2 [41]. The surface properties of AZG films ob­tained at different carbonization temperatures were evaluated through water contact angle (WCA) measurements. As illustrated in Fig. 3b, the WCA of the carbonized samples at both 550 ◦C and 700 ◦C were below 90◦, confirming their hydrophilic property. Notably, the WCA decreased from 57.0◦for AZG-550 to 28.5◦for AZG-700, indicating a significant enhancement in hydrophilicity with increasing carbonization tempera­ture. This increase in hydrophilicity can be attributed to two factors: the complete pyrolysis of organic components and the formation of N-doped graphitic carbon structures, which inherently exhibit superior hydro­philic characteristics. Having elucidated the microstructure and surface properties of the AZG materials carbonized at different temperatures, their PV dehydra­tion performance was predicted using vapor-phase sorption isotherms. Fig. 3c and d presents the adsorption isotherms of H2O and MeOH on the carbonized AZG xerogel powders within the relative pressure (P/Ps) range of 0–0.4. In this pressure range, adsorption predominantly occurs within the micropores and mesopores of the samples. Both AZG-550 and AZG-700 exhibited significantly higher adsorption capacities for H2O compared to MeOH, a result attributable to their hydrophilic nature.

citation.title: Hydrothermally robust carbon-silica-zirconia ceramic membranes for efficient pervaporation dehydration

content: AZG-550 and AZG-700 exhibited significantly higher adsorption capacities for H2O compared to MeOH, a result attributable to their hydrophilic nature. However, the adsorption capacities of AZG-700 for H2O and MeOH were lower than those of AZG-550. Fig. S2 presents the isosteric heats of H2O and MeOH adsorption on AZG-550 and AZG-700 xerogel powders. Although AZG-700 exhibited lower adsorption capacities for both H2O and MeOH, its adsorption heats were higher than those of AZG-550, indicating its stronger H2O interactions. Furthermore, the H2O adsorp­tion capacity was normalized based on specific surface areas to provide a more accurate comparison of the intrinsic hydrophilicity of the two materials. As shown in Table S2, AZG-700 (6.25 cm3 m-2) exhibited a higher normalized adsorption capacity than AZG-550 (1.65 cm3 m-2), indicating stronger hydrophilic interaction in the AZG-700 sample. This suggests that the lower overall adsorption capacity of AZG-700 is pri­marily due to its denser structure, which limits accessible adsorption sites. In contrast, the AZG-550 combines high porosity and moderate hydrophilicity, leading to enhanced adsorption performance and potentially superior PV dehydration efficiency.

citation.title: Hydrothermally robust carbon-silica-zirconia ceramic membranes for efficient pervaporation dehydration

content: 3.2. PV dehydration performance of carbonized AZG membranes Fig. S3 shows the surface and cross-sectional SEM images of the AZG550 and AZG-700 membranes. The continuous SiO2–ZrO2 composite ceramic network constitutes the main structure of the separation layer, with the carbon generated during pyrolysis confined within this network structure, ensuring the formation of a smooth separation layer. Furthermore, coating with the particle and intermediate layers covered the pinholes and defects. Therefore, the membranes exhibited a continuous, defect-free morphology with a smooth AZG-selective layer

citation.title: Hydrothermally robust carbon-silica-zirconia ceramic membranes for efficient pervaporation dehydration

content: Fig. 3. (a) CO2 adsorption isotherms of AZG-550 and AZG-700 xerogel powders at 25 ◦C. (b) WCA AZG-550 and AZG-700 films. (c) H2O and (d) MeOH adsorption isotherms of AZG-550 and AZG-700 xerogel powders at 25 ◦C. X. Niu et al. Journal of Membrane Science 730 (2025) 124197 adhering to the intermediate layer. As demonstrated in our previous studies, cross-linked organic precursors within the AZG network exhibit reduced mobility, which promotes the uniform distribution of free car­bon and effectively minimizes defect formation [29]. Notably, an in­crease in carbonization temperature resulted in a reduction in the thickness of the AZG separation layer from 90 nm for AZG-550 to 60 nm for AZG-700. This reduction in thickness can be attributed to the extensive degradation of the organic components and densification of the SiO2–ZrO2 network during high-temperature calcination. To investigate the effects of the microstructure and surface charac­teristics on PV dehydration performance at different carbonization temperatures, the PV time courses of AZG-550 and AZG-700 membranes were evaluated in various H2O/solvent (10/90 wt %) mixtures at 50 ◦C, as shown in Fig. 4. The permeance and selectivity, which exclude the impact of driving force variations, offer a reliable and consistent basis for the comparative assessment of PV performance across the mem­branes.

citation.title: Hydrothermally robust carbon-silica-zirconia ceramic membranes for efficient pervaporation dehydration

content: the impact of driving force variations, offer a reliable and consistent basis for the comparative assessment of PV performance across the mem­branes. Despite the greater membrane thickness, the AZG-550 mem­brane exhibited superior permeance compared with the AZG-700 membrane in both pure H2O and mixed solvent systems. This indicates that the performance disparity is primarily attributed to variations in the network structures and surface chemistry resulting from different carbonization temperatures. While the selectivities in the H2O/MeOH system were comparable between AZG-550 and AZG-700 membranes, the AZG-550 membrane demonstrated a significantly higher selectivity for the H2O/EtOH, H2O/acetone, and H2O/IPA mixtures. In particular, the AZG-550 membrane exhibited an H2O permeance of 2.1 * 10-6 mol m-2 s-1 Pa-1 and a selectivity of 2000 for EtOH dehydration. Notably, no detectable IPA signal was observed in the permeate of the H2O/IPA experiment for the AZG-550 membrane. However, based on chromato­graphic accuracy, the selectivity for H2O/IPA exceeds 10000. The superior permeance and separation performance of the AZG-550 membrane can be attributed to its relatively loose network structure and approperiate hydrophilicity. The loose structure minimizes resistance to H2O permeation, while the hydrophilic nature promotes H2O adsorption onto the pore walls.

citation.title: Hydrothermally robust carbon-silica-zirconia ceramic membranes for efficient pervaporation dehydration

content: hydrophilicity. The loose structure minimizes resistance to H2O permeation, while the hydrophilic nature promotes H2O adsorption onto the pore walls. This facilitates the alignment of H2O molecules into a continuous chain within the pores, enabling the formation of a rapid transport pathway for H2O molecules via hydrogen bonding interactions [42]. This process also effectively reduces the effective membrane pore size, thereby restricting the permeation of solvent molecules and enhancing the selective separation of H2O from the solvent [43]. In contrast, although AZG-700 exhibits higher hydrophilicity, its denser structure inhibits the formation of the rapid H2O permeation layer within the pores. Consequently, the AZG-700 membrane primarily relies on its intrinsic pore size for H2O-solvent separation, resulting in lower permeance and selectivity compared to the AZG-550 membrane.

citation.title: Hydrothermally robust carbon-silica-zirconia ceramic membranes for efficient pervaporation dehydration

content: The H2O permeance of the membrane in binary systems is lower than that in pure H2O, primarily due to the adsorbed/diffused solvent mol­ecules within the membrane pores, which restrict the effective perme­ation of H2O. For the AZG-700 membrane, the H2O permeances in the H2O/acetone and H2O/IPA systems are higher than those in H2O/MeOH and H2O/EtOH systems, respectively. This phenomenon can be attrib­uted to the larger molecular size of acetone and IPA, both of which mainly permeate through large pores of the membrane, such as pinholes, thereby releasing micropores that are more conducive to the permeation of H2O molecules. According to our previous study, pinholes exhibit a greater impact on the performance of membranes with dense network structures [44]. As a result, the H2O permeance of the AZG-550 mem­brane, characterized by its loose structure, remained relatively stable across various binary systems. Fig. 5a and b compare the molecular size dependence of permeance for the AZG-550 and AZG-700 membranes in both single-gas permeation and PV dehydration processes. Given the influence of pinholes on H2O permeance in H2O/acetone and H2O/IPA mixtures for the AZG-700 membrane, H2O permeance in H2O/MeOH mixtures was selected for comparison. Both membranes exhibited higher H2O (0.2955 nm) per­meance than H2 (0.289 nm) despite their similar molecular sizes, which can be reasonably attributed to surface diffusion.

citation.title: Hydrothermally robust carbon-silica-zirconia ceramic membranes for efficient pervaporation dehydration

content: d higher H2O (0.2955 nm) per­meance than H2 (0.289 nm) despite their similar molecular sizes, which can be reasonably attributed to surface diffusion. Notably, the PV mo­lecular size dependence curve for AZG-700 closely aligned the gas permeation results, with the selectivity of H2O/EtOH (320) resembling that of H2/CH4 (230). This suggests that the PV performance of the AZG700 membrane is predominantly governed by molecular sieving effects. In contrast, the PV molecular size dependence curve of AZG-550 exhibited a steeper trend compared to that observed for gases, with a significantly higher selectivity of H2O/EtOH (2000) than that of H2/CH4 (120). This indicates superior PV dehydration separation performance and further suggests that its separation ability is influenced by addi­tional factors beyond the molecular sieving properties imparted by its intrinsic pore structure. The PV dehydration performance of the membranes is governed by their microporous structures and surface characteristics. To further elucidate the evolution of pore characteristics in the AZG membranes at various carbonization temperatures, the normalized Knudsen-based permeance (NKP) was employed as a function of the kinetic diameter to estimate membrane pore size, as detailed in Text S2 of the Supporting Information. The linear plots of the normalized Knudsen-based per­meance (NKP) versus the kinetic diameter for the AZG-550 and AZG-700 membranes are depicted in Fig. 5c.

citation.title: Hydrothermally robust carbon-silica-zirconia ceramic membranes for efficient pervaporation dehydration

content: near plots of the normalized Knudsen-based per­meance (NKP) versus the kinetic diameter for the AZG-550 and AZG-700 membranes are depicted in Fig. 5c. As the carbonization temperature increased from 550 ◦C to 700 ◦C, the membrane pore size decreased from 0.55 nm to 0.41 nm. This reduction is attributed to the densifica­tion of the network structure, which accounts for the reduced permeance

content: Fig. 4. Time courses for PV dehydration performance in H2O/solvent (10/90 wt %) mixtures at 50 ◦C for (a) AZG-550 and (b) AZG-700 membranes. X. Niu et al. Journal of Membrane Science 730 (2025) 124197 observed in the AZG-700 membrane. To gain a more comprehensive understanding of the PV dehydration mechanism, the temperature dependence of the permeance of H2 and pure H2O through the AZG-550 and AZG-700 membranes was analyzed, as illustrated in Fig. 5d. The activation energies for H2 and H2O permeation were also determined (detailed in Texts S2 and S3). In contrast, the H2O permeance exhibited a decreasing trend with increasing temperature, indicating the predominance of an adsorptiondominated permeation mechanism. This behavior suggests that H2O permeation is primarily governed by the adsorption-diffusion or surface diffusion. The activation energy for H2 permeance in the AZG-700 membrane (20.2 kJ mol-1) is significantly higher than that of the AZG-550 membrane (7.5 kJ mol-1), which can be attributed to its smaller pore size. Additionally, the absolute value of the activation en­ergy for H2O permeance in the AZG-700 membrane (-6.1 kJ mol-1) is lower than that of the AZG-550 membrane (-14.8 kJ mol-1), indicating that H2O permeation in the AZG-700 membrane is less significantly influenced by adsorption process.

citation.title: Hydrothermally robust carbon-silica-zirconia ceramic membranes for efficient pervaporation dehydration

content: the AZG-550 membrane (-14.8 kJ mol-1), indicating that H2O permeation in the AZG-700 membrane is less significantly influenced by adsorption process. The apparent activation energy of permeation is defined as follows: EP,i = ED,i + ΔHads,i(2) where EP,i, ED,i, and ΔHads,i represent the apparent activation energy, activation energy of diffusion, and adsorption enthalpy of the i-th component, respectively. Considering the similarity in molecular size between the H2 and H2O the activation energies of diffusion within the membrane are expected to be comparable [45]. Additionally, adsorption enthalpy of non-adsorptive H2 on the membrane surface is negligible. Consequently, the adsorption enthalpy of H2O molecules on the mem­brane surface can be determined as follows:

citation.title: Hydrothermally robust carbon-silica-zirconia ceramic membranes for efficient pervaporation dehydration

content: ΔHads,H2O = EP,H2O -ED,H2O ≈EP,H2O -ED,H2 ≈EP,H2O -EP,H2(3) The adsorption enthalpies of H2O on the surfaces of AZG-550 and AZG-700 membranes were determined to be -22.3 kJ mol-1 and -26.3 kJ mol-1, respectively, further confirming the stronger H2O affinity of the AZG-700 membrane. However, the increased diffusion resistance resulting from its denser structure reduces the overall contribution of adsorption to its PV performance. In contrast, the performance of the AZG-550 membrane, characterized by its loose network structure and moderate hydrophilicity, was more significantly influenced by the adsorption-diffusion process. Notably, both the AZG-550 and AZG-700 membranes maintained structural and performance integrity after pro­longed exposure to pure H2O at 40–60 ◦C, demonstrating outstanding hydrothermal stability. The temperature dependence of the AZG-550 and AZG-700 mem­branes in H2O/alcohol (MeOH and EtOH) dehydration is presented in Fig. 6, with the corresponding activation energies listed in Table 1. For the AZG-550 membrane, the permeances of H2O, MeOH, and EtOH exhibited a slight decline as temperature increased, suggesting that the permeation process was primarily adsorption-dominated. Due to its hydrophilic nature, the absolute value of the activation energy for H2O was higher than those for MeOH and EtOH. In contrast, the AZG-700 membrane demonstrated only a minimal decrease in H2O permeance with increasing temperature.

For MeOH and EtOH, the permeance exhibited a slight increase with higher temperature, indicating activated diffusion behavior. The absolute value of activation energy for H2O in the H2O/MeOH system was lower than that in H2O/EtOH for both membranes. This can be attributed to the interaction between alcohol and H2O molecules, leading to the formation of molecular clusters

(coupling effects). These clusters obstruct the adsorption sites of H2O, thereby hindering its preferential adsorption and diffusion within the membrane [46]. Since alcohol contains only a single hydroxyl group, the coupling effect weakens as the carbon chain length increases (EtOH <MeOH). The H2O/MeOH selectivity of the AZG-550 membrane was lower than that of the AZG-700 membrane, whereas the H2O/EtOH selectivity was higher. This difference can be attributed to the stronger affinity of

citation.title: Hydrothermally robust carbon-silica-zirconia ceramic membranes for efficient pervaporation dehydration

content:
MeOH [47], with its higher polarity, for the hydrophilic membrane surface compared with that of EtOH and its stronger coupling effect with H2O. Consequently, the adsorption-diffusion gives a limited contribu­tion to H2O/MeOH separation by the AZG-550 membrane. Furthermore, the weaker sieving ability of the AZG-550 membrane compared with the AZG-700 membrane resulted in lower H2O/MeOH selectivity. In contrast, the larger polarity difference and weaker coupling effect be­tween EtOH and H2O enabled the adsorption-diffusion process to play a more prominent role in H2O/EtOH separation. This, in turn, leads to the higher selectivity of the AZG-550 membrane compared with that of the AZG-700 membrane. Notably, the AZG-550 membrane maintained high H2O permeances (>10-6 mol m-2 s-1 Pa-1) and selectivities of H2O/MeOH >130 and H2O/EtOH >1400 across varying temperature conditions. Fig. 7a illustrates the effect of the feed concentration on the PV dehydration performance of H2O/EtOH mixtures (H2O concentrations from 1 wt % to 90 wt %) at 50 ◦C for AZG-550 and AZG-700 membranes. During EtOH dehydration, the AZG-550 membrane exhibited a gradual increase in both H2O and EtOH permeances with increasing H2O content in the feed. However, the selectivities remained largely unchanged. This

Journal of Membrane Science 730 (2025) 124197 phenomenon can be attributed to a reduction in the absorption or diffusion of alcohol within the membrane pores, thereby increasing the effective number of pores available for H2O permeation. In contrast, for the AZG-700 membrane, the permeances of H2O and EtOH, as well as the selectivities, exhibited minimal variation with changes in the feedside H2O content. This further suggests that the permeation behavior is largely independent of the adsorption process. Notably, the AZG-550 membrane exhibited H2O permeances higher than 10-6 mol m-2 s-1 Pa-1 and H2O/EtOH selectivities exceeding 2000 within the tested concentration range. Fig. 7b illustrates the correlation between the H2/CH4 and H2O/ EtOH permeation ratios for silica-based membranes derived from the sol-gel process (membrane data are detailed in Table S3), as the permeable and non-permeable species exhibit similar molecular sizes [43]. The selectivities of the AZG-550 and AZG-700 membranes across varying feed-side H2O contents aligned with the correlation line, emphasizing the dominant role of molecular sieving in H2O/alcohol separation.

citation.title: Hydrothermally robust carbon-silica-zirconia ceramic membranes for efficient pervaporation dehydration

content: across varying feed-side H2O contents aligned with the correlation line, emphasizing the dominant role of molecular sieving in H2O/alcohol separation. However, the AZG-550 membrane, though characterized by a looser structure, exhibited a higher selectivity due to its enhanced sieving capability, further facilitated by the adsorbed/diffused H2O molecules. Fig. S4 presents the correlation between the H2 and H2O permeances (membrane data are detailed in Table S4), providing addi­tional insight into the transport mechanisms governing these mem­branes [43]. As the H2O content on the feed side increased, the H2O permeance of the AZG-550 membrane increased significantly, deviating from the correlation line, whereas the change in the AZG-700 membrane was relatively small and remained consistent with the correlation line. These results further confirm that the PV performance of the AZG-550 membrane is governed by both the adsorption-diffusion mechanism and molecular sieving. In contrast, the performance of the AZG-700 membrane is primarily dictated by molecular sieving. These findings underscore the significance of the loose pore structure of the membrane in enhancing the contribution of its hydrophilic surface to PV dehy­dration performance. In the future, more systematic studies will be conducted to establish more refined screening criteria for PV dehydra­tion membranes. 3.3.

content: performance. In the future, more systematic studies will be conducted to establish more refined screening criteria for PV dehydra­tion membranes. 3.3. Effect of carbon on hydrothermal stability of carbon-SiO2-ZrO2 membrane In the previous section, the hydrothermal stabilities of AZG-550 and AZG-700 membranes in pure H2O were confirmed. To further evaluate the underlying reasons for the stability of the carbon-SiO2-ZrO2 network, the AZG-550 xerogel powder and membrane were subjected to ozone (O3) treatment at 200 ◦C (denoted as AZG-550 O3; the procedure is detailed in Text S4 of the Supporting Information) to remove free carbon. The properties of the O3-treated samples were then compared to those of the untreated AZG-550 to assess the impact of carbon on membrane performance and structural stability. The XPS analysis revealed characteristic peaks for Si 2p, Zr 3d, and C 1s at 102.7, 184.7, and 285.8 eV, respectively, for both samples. In the AZG-550 O3 sample, the intensity of the C 1s peak significantly decreased, with the C/Si atomic ratio reducing from 0.80 (AZG-550) to 0.35 (AZG-550 O3), indicating effective carbon removal. The structural changes induced by O3 treatment were further analyzed by N2 adsorption/desorption. As shown in Fig. 8b, both sam­ples exhibited type I isotherms, confirming their microporous structures.

citation.title: Hydrothermally robust carbon-silica-zirconia ceramic membranes for efficient pervaporation dehydration

content: further analyzed by N2 adsorption/desorption. As shown in Fig. 8b, both sam­ples exhibited type I isotherms, confirming their microporous structures. Compared to AZG-550, AZG-550 O3 sample exhibited an increased N2 adsorption capacity, Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) surface area, and total pore volume (Table 2), indicating a more porous structure resulting from the due to the released of network space by the removal of free carbon. Furthermore, the pore size distribution derived from non-local density functional theory (NLDFT) revealed that AZG-550 O3 possessed a bimodal pore size distribution, as shown in Fig. S5.

citation.title: Hydrothermally robust carbon-silica-zirconia ceramic membranes for efficient pervaporation dehydration

content: Fig. 8c presents the WCA results and water adsorption isotherms for the AZG-550 samples before and after O3 treatment. The WCA of AZG550 O3 (17.3◦) was significantly lower than that of AZG-550 (57.0◦), indicating enhanced hydrophilicity. AZG-550 O3 exhibited a higher water adsorption capacity compared to untreated AZG-550, attributed to its increased porosity and more pronounced hydrophilicity. However, as shown in Fig. 8d, AZG-550 O3 also exhibited a higher MeOH uptake than AZG-550, likely due to its looser structure. After elucidating the reduction in carbon content of AZG-550 following O3 treatment and its impact on the structure and surface properties, we further investigated the PV performance and stability of the membranes under varying operating temperatures and hydrother­mal conditions. As illustrated in Fig. 9a and b and Fig. S6, the AZG-550 O3 membrane demonstrated superior H2O permeance compared to AZG550 in H2O/EtOH (10/90 wt %) and H2O/MeOH (10/90 wt %) PV dehydration. This enhancement can be attributed to its increased hy­drophilicity and more porous structure. However, the selectivities of the AZG-550 O3 membrane were lower than those of AZG-550, which can be attributed to a reduction in molecular sieving ability due to its looser network structure. Both AZG-550 and AZG-550 O3 membranes exhibited stable PV performance during MeOH dehydration (30–60 ◦C) and EtOH dehydration (40–70 ◦C) due to the relative stable SiO2–ZrO2 network structure.

citation.title: Hydrothermally robust carbon-silica-zirconia ceramic membranes for efficient pervaporation dehydration

content: bited stable PV performance during MeOH dehydration (30–60 ◦C) and EtOH dehydration (40–70 ◦C) due to the relative stable SiO2–ZrO2 network structure. Furthermore, after undergoing hydrothermal treatment in pure water at 90 ◦C for 12 h, the separation performance of AZG-550 remained virtually unchanged, highlighting its excellent hydrothermal stability. In contrast, the permeance of the AZG-550 O3 membrane increased, while its selectivity decreased after hydrothermal treatment, likely due to the enlargement of pore size caused by Si dissolution in water [48]. This observation suggests that the presence of free carbon played a crucial role in enhancing the stability of the C–SiO2–ZrO2 membranes. Based on the aforementioned results, we propose the mechanism elucidating the role of free carbon in enhancing the PV performance and stability of the carbonized AZG membranes, as illustrated in Fig. 9c. Firstly, the presence of free carbon within the network structure pro­motes the formation of uniformly distributed micropores, thereby improving the molecular sieving capability of the membrane. Secondly, the hydrophilic N-doped graphitic carbon facilitates the selective adsorption and diffusion of H2O molecules within the membrane pores while effectively excluding alcohol molecules. More importantly, the presence of free carbon serves as a protective role in preventing the collapse of the SiO2–ZrO2 network under hydrothermal conditions by shielding the ceramic network structure.

citation.title: Hydrothermally robust carbon-silica-zirconia ceramic membranes for efficient pervaporation dehydration

content: Fig. 9. The PV dehydration performance in H2O/EtOH (10/90 wt %) at 40–70 ◦C for (a) AZG-550 and (b) AZG-550 O3 membranes before and after hydrothermal treatment at 90 ◦C in pure water. (c) A schematic illusion depicting the proposed mechanism of free carbon in enhancing membrane performance and stability. X. Niu et al. Journal of Membrane Science 730 (2025) 124197 permeance but reduced selectivity due to its highly hydrophilic surface and loose network structure. Furthermore, direct exposure of the ceramic network to severe hydrothermal conditions resulted in a decline in membrane stability, highlighting the critical role of free carbon in maintaining the structural integrity and separation performance of AZG membranes. In previous results, we demonstrated the stability of the PV dehy­dration performance of AZG-550 membranes across various tempera­tures and H2O contents, underscoring their potential for practical applications. Subsequently, the long-term stability of the membranes was evaluated. As shown in Fig. 10a, the H2O/EtOH PV dehydration performance of the AZG-550 membrane exhibited a slight decline within the initial 1 h, after which it remained stable during continuous opera­tion for up to 12 h. Moreover, the PV performance of the membrane remained stable during continuous operation for 14 h following 28 d of storage in EtOH at room temperature, demonstrating exceptional operational stability. Furthermore, Fig.

citation.title: Hydrothermally robust carbon-silica-zirconia ceramic membranes for efficient pervaporation dehydration

content: ontinuous operation for 14 h following 28 d of storage in EtOH at room temperature, demonstrating exceptional operational stability. Furthermore, Fig. 10b–f compare the H2O/MeOH and H2O/EtOH PV dehydration performances of the carbonized AZG membranes with those of the state-of-the-art membranes reported in the literature (detailed data are provided in Tables S5 and S6). Owing to the optimal combination of hydrophilicity and high microporosity, the AZG550 membrane exhibited superior H2O permeance and flux for H2O/ MeOH and H2O/EtOH, as well as a higher selectivity and separation factor for H2O/EtOH, surpassing those of the AZG-700 membrane. During the PV dehydration of H2O/MeOH (with the H2O content ranging from 10 to 70 wt %), the AZG-550 membrane exhibited H2O permeance of (2.8–6.4) * 10-6 mol m-2 s-1 Pa-1 and an H2O/MeOH selectivity of 140–190 as well as an H2O flux of 0.54–4.26 kg m-2 h-1 and an H2O/MeOH separation factor of 30–50. Similarly, during PV dehydration in H2O/EtOH (with the H2O content ranging from 10 to 70 wt %), the AZG-550 membrane demonstrated an H2O permeance of (2.1–6.8) * 10-6 mol m-2 s-1 Pa-1 and an H2O/EtOH selectivity of 2000–2700 as well as an H2O flux of 0.85–4.95 kg m-2 h-1 and an H2O/ EtOH separation factor of 390–1820.
